title: Roast Poussin
subtitle: A young bird buttered under the skin and roasted in 35 minutes, one per person
excerpt: Poussin are young chickens weighing 400-500g, sold whole and roasted one per person. They cook in 35 minutes at high heat, need very little seasoning, and are a neater and more elegant plate than a carved roast chicken.
sourceNotes: Traditional British and French roasting practice. Fanny Cradock, The Ambitious Cook (1970s): poussin roasting guide. Jane Grigson, English Food (1974): small bird timing and presentation.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Butter can go under the skin up to 24 hours ahead. Cover and refrigerate; bring to room temperature 30 minutes before roasting.

glossaryTerms.definition: A young chicken of 4 to 6 weeks old, weighing 400 to 500 g and sold whole. One bird serves one person; the flesh is pale, fine-grained, and milder than a grown bird. The French word is standard in British butchery.
Butterflying a bird by removing the backbone and pressing it flat so the breast and legs lie at the same level, giving even cooking at high heat. An alternative to roasting whole.

content.text: is the most forgiving roast you can cook: a bird so small that from cold oven door to resting board takes under an hour, and the margin for error is wide. Butter under the skin keeps the breast moist; a hot oven of 220°C gives colour quickly enough that the legs cook through before the breast dries.
One bird per person makes serving straightforward. Each diner gets a whole roasted bird, needing no carving at the table. The alternative is to
them flat, which reduces cooking time by 10 minutes and gives crispier skin all over, but the whole bird is more elegant for a dinner.

items.prepNote: poussin, approx. 450-500 g each, at room temperature

content.text: Heat the oven to 220°C (200°C fan). Pat each bird dry. Ease the skin from the breast by working your fingers from the neck opening down toward the wishbone. Push
under the skin, dividing it evenly between the two birds. Season the skin with
. Tuck a sprig of thyme and a slice of lemon inside each cavity, along with a garlic clove if using.

Set the birds breast-up in the roasting pan, not touching. Roast for 30-35 minutes until the skin is deep golden and the juices run clear when you pierce the thigh. A probe at the thickest part of the leg should read 75°C. Transfer to a warm plate and rest for 10 minutes before serving. The resting is brief; small birds lose heat quickly.
Tip off most of the fat from the roasting pan. Add
and scrape up any fond over medium heat. Simmer for 2 minutes, season, and strain into a warm jug.

rows.cause: Oven was not fully preheated, or butter on the skin surface is preventing browning
rows.fix: Check with an oven thermometer. A 5-minute blast at 240°C at the end will colour pale skin quickly without overcooking the flesh.

rows.cause: Birds were cold from the fridge, or larger than 500 g
rows.fix: Return to the oven for 5-minute increments. Always probe rather than relying on time. Cold birds take 5-10 minutes longer.

rows.cause: Overcooked or butter did not distribute evenly
rows.fix: Pull birds at 75°C on the probe. Make sure butter covers both breast fillets, not just one side.

rows.cause: Bird not at room temperature before roasting
rows.fix: Rest birds at room temperature for 30 minutes before the oven.

content.text: Poussin fell out of fashion in British home cooking but remains a restaurant staple. They are particularly useful for a two-person dinner where a full roast chicken produces more leftovers than the occasion warrants. The cooking method is identical to a standard roast chicken; the scale is simply smaller, and the result is tidier.
